description:
Curated examples designed to teach you the essentials of adrenal MRI.
Thank you to Dr. Satheesh Krishna, expert GU radiologist, for help with this course.

cases.clinicalHistory: r/o adrenal metastasis. Incidental nodule (15 HU non-contrast)

series.label: AX T1 In Out Phase bh 3mm 70flip

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Right adrenal nodule
  • Contains homogeneous definite intravoxel fat (in-phase vs out-of-phase). Also notice fatty liver on same sequences.
  • Measures 4.1 cm
  • Some mild T2W cystic change centrally

This was resected due to its large size and came back as an adrenal adenoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Adrenal adenomas are benign adrenocortical neoplasms. They are common incidental lesions. Though benign, they can sometimes be hormonally active (Cushing's, hypertension, etc).

On CT, lipid-rich adenomas are often homogeneous, and have a non-contrast attenuation of <10HU -- considered diagnostic of an adenoma. When attenuation is higher, the diagnosis of lipid-rich adenoma cannot be made (here, it was 15HU).

On MRI, chemical shift MR (in-and-out of phase imaging) is the most reliable way to diagnose adenomas. Adenomas contain lipid and will demonstrate drop in signal on out-of-phase images (intravoxel fat).

Rarely, adenomas can have some cystic degeneration or hemorrhage.


NOTE: larger adrenal lesions over 4 cm (apart from myelolipomas) have an increased chance of malignancy. This includes those that have typical appearances of adrenocortical lesions/adenomas. So, the classic teaching is that >4 cm solid adrenal nodules (apart from myelolipomas) should be considered for resection. Many may still be adenomas (like this case).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Large right adrenal mass (14.3 cm)
  • T1W hyperintense and suppressed on FS sequences (i.e. see here, here, here - recall that DWI is inherently fat suppressed). Thus this mass is composed of predominantly bulk fat.

This is diagnostic of an adrenal myelolipoma

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Adrenal myelolipomas are benign, non-functioning tumors that contain bulk fat and hematopoietic tissue.

The most important takeaway here is that they are characterized by presence of bulk fat. A bulk fat containing adrenal mass should be considered diagnostic a myelolipoma, with few exceptions.

The main exception you should know is that adrenocortical lesions can undergo 'myelolipomatous degeneration' -- i.e. an adenoma or adrenocortical carcinoma can have tiny foci of bulk fat within that represent myelolipomatous degeneration. This will appear as a typical adenoma or ACC, but with a very tiny area of bulk fat. That is in contrast to masses predominantly composed of bulk-fat - like this case.

Clinical Knowledge:

In the vast majority of cases, myelolipomas have NO clinical significance -- they are benign and asymptomatic. However, very large myelolipomas (> 7-10 cm) can cause mass effect, and also have increased risk of bleeding. This is VERY rare, but worth knowing about. Hence, very large myelolipomas may be considered for surgical resection (> 7 cm is the classic teaching). I've seen 1000s of myelolipomas, but only few very large ones, and only a single case of hemorrhage so far in my career.

Also important: myelolipomas are an exception to the 4 cm rule for adrenal nodules introduced in the last case. Specifically, solid adrenal masses > 4 cm have an increased risk of malignancy and so are generally considered for resection -- even if typical of adenoma. Again, the exception is myelolipoma (i.e. even this very large one is definitively benign, and would only be resected for the increased risk of hemorrhage).

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 3 - Metastases from RCC (containing intravoxel fat)

series.label: AX T1 dixon vibe caipi4 bh_in
AX T1 dixon vibe caipi4 bh_opp
Ax T2 haste spair TE 80 p2 mbh
AX T1 dixon vibe caipi4 bh_F
AX T1 dixon vibe caipi4 bh_W
COR T1 PRE vibe fs p2 bh
COR T1 DYNAMIC vibe fs p2 bh
COR T1 DYNAMIC vibe fs p2 bh
COR T1 DYNAMIC vibe fs p2 bh
AX T1 DELAY vibe dixon caipi4 bh_W
SAG T1 DELAY vibe dixon caipi4 bh_W

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Multiple left adrenal nodules, best seen post-gad (e.g. here, here, here, etc)
  • Many of these contain intravoxel fat -- compare in-phase with out-of-phase. Also see this one
  • Post-gad shows they are hypervascular (see above)
  • History of right nephrectomy/adrenalectomy (absent; also see surgical clips)

These adrenal nodules represent clear cell RCC metastases.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This case demonstrates that some types of adrenal metastases CAN contain intravoxel fat. Most commonly, clear cell RCC (we demonstrated that these often contain intravoxel fat in renal MRI course), and HCC. If unaware, one may inappropriately call metastatic disease to be 'adenomas'.

Thus, while the presence of intravoxel fat in a homogeneous adrenal nodule < 4 cm is diagnostic of an adrenal adenoma most of the time -- the most important exception is metastases from certain primaries. But, you would only consider metastases if there is a known history of (or presence of) one of those tumors (i.e. RCC, HCC). In this case, there was a history of right nephrectomy for clear cell RCC, and these hypervascular adrenal nodules developed on follow-up -- and so diagnostic of adrenal metastases.

series.label: AX T1 VIBE POST GAD ARTERIAL
AX T1 VIBE POST GAD ARTERIAL_SUB
AX T1 VIBE POST GAD EARLY DELAY
AX T1 VIBE POST GAD EARLY DELAY_SUB
AX T1 VIBE POST GAD VENOUS
AX T1 VIBE POST GAD VENOUS_SUB

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Left adrenal mass measuring 4.7 cm:

  • Heterogeneous on T2W with some areas of relative hyperintensity (also see coronal)
  • Intravoxel fat containing (compare in-phase and out-of-phase)

This was resected and came back as ACC (Adrenocortical Carcinoma)

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) is a malignant adrenocortical neoplasm, is rare, and generally has a poor prognosis. Though often aggressive, they exist along a spectrum. Many are functional so patients present with symptoms (i.e. Cushing's).

The key learning points here are twofold: knowing the imaging appearance of ACC in general (in addition to this example), and knowing that for solid lesions > 4 cm that are not myelolipomas -- surgical resection is classically considered. This is because over 4 cm, the chance of malignancy increases.


Imaging Appearance:

  • Large (often much larger than this one, but generally > 4 cm)
  • Heterogeneous (nonspecific, but often quite heterogeneous with necrosis, hemorrhage, etc).
  • Invasion of surrounding structures -- ACC likes to invade vein (i.e. IVC, renal vein, etc)
  • Intravoxel fat is not uncommon
  • Rarely, can have small foci of bulk fat (myelolipomatous degeneration, introduced in a prior case)


Remember, adenomas tend to be more homogeneous and often smaller. For nodules with intravoxel fat, the larger and more heterogeneous the nodule, the more likely it is malignant (i.e. ACC). Though we use 4 cm as a cutoff to consider resection -- many tumors slightly larger than 4 cm resected will end up being benign adenomas (like the one we showed earlier), and others may be lower grade ACCs (like this one). More aggressive ACCs often demonstrate clear malignant behavior (i.e. vein invasion).

series.label: 3D AX DRY RUN FT LAVA ASSET

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Left adrenal nodule measuring about 3.2 cm, with mild T2 hyperintensity
  • Diffusion restricting (DWI, ADC)
  • Some cystic change
  • Relatively avid enhancement
  • No intravoxel fat (compare in and out)

After discovering this adrenal nodule, serum metanephrines were drawn that were elevated. This was resected and confirmed to be a pheochromocytoma

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Pheochromocytoma are rare neuroendocrine neoplasms arising from adrenal medulla. Most are benign, with about 10% malignant. They often secrete catecholamines (epi, norepi), hence classic symptoms (HTN, palpitations, etc). Serum and urine metanephrines have high sensitivity and specificity for pheo.


Imaging appearance:

  • Variable -- can have any of cystic degeneration, necrosis, calcification, and intravoxel fat (rarely). Given variability, has been called an "imaging chameleon"
  • Usually 3-5 cm but can be larger
  • Marked T2W hyperintensity ("light bulb" appearance) is often mentioned, but present in vast minority (only 10%), but T2 hyperintensity is present in about 2/3 (like our case)
  • Common pattern: heterogeneously enhancing lesion with small cystic pockets (like this case)
  • MOST do NOT have intravoxel fat (vast majority), but can rarely occur -- usually in a small portion of the lesion and mild.
  • Often enhances avidly, but not always

At the end of the day, all patients with incidental adrenal nodule should get serum (or urine) metanephrines -- as this is HIGHLY sensitive and specific for pheo. However, in real life, this doesn't always happen.

Thus, the role of the radiologist is to raise the possibility in appropriate cases, provide a Ddx, localize the tumor, characterize it, and look for local invasion/metastases.

cases.clinicalHistory: Right Adrenal Nodule on CT, post-op

series.label: Cor t2 haste TE180 p3 bh
Ax t2 haste spair TE 80 p2 bh
Cor t2 MRCP space rst caipi4 fs trigger
Cor t2 MRCP space rst caipi4 fs trigger_MIP_COR
Ax t1 vibe dixon caipi4 bh_opp
Ax t1 vibe dixon caipi4 bh_W
Ax t1 vibe dixon caipi4 bh_in
Ax t1 vibe dixon caipi4 bh_F
Ax dwi ep2d b50 400 800 fs p2_ADC
Ax dwi ep2d b50 400 800 fs p2_TRACEW
Ax t1 pre vibe spair p2 bh
Ax t1 dyn arterial vibe spair p2 bh
Ax t1 dyn portal venous vibe spair p2 bh
Ax t1 dyn delayed venous vibe spair p2 bh
Cor t1 delay vibe dixon caipi5 bh_W
Ax t1 delay vibe dixon caipi3 1.5mm bh_W
Ax t1 delay vibe dixon caipi3 1.5mm bh_W_COMPOSED
Ax t1 delay vibe dixon caipi3 1.5mm bh_W

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Recent liver transplant (notice surgical clips on in-phase), abdominal wall incision,
  • Right adrenal nodule measuring 2 cm demonstrating T2W hyperintensity
  • Clear T1W hyperintensity within indicating hemorrhagic or proteinaceous material
  • No internal enhancement (subtraction images would have been ideal)

Findings in this clinical setting are in keeping with an adrenal hemorrhage/hematoma.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Adrenal hemorrhage often occurs after trauma or iatrogenic (post surgical) -- due to direct injury or increased venous pressure. Other predisposing factors include: blood thinners, physiological stress (surgery, burns, sepsis, etc), pregnancy.

The vast majority are right sided (about 80%), like this case. They will often resolve over time, or result in calcification/residual cystic change.

In this case, patient had a recent liver transplant.


The role of MRI is to cement the diagnosis and exclude an underlying mass. MRI signal depends on stage of blood (acute is more like fluid, subacute is T1 hyperintense, chronic demonstrates hemosiderin), with lack of internal enhancement / no solid enhancing mass. The clinical history is also critical -- i.e. presence of predisposing factors.


The main thing to know is that adrenal hemorrhage can mimic other masses on imaging. Underlying lesions can also bleed, including myelolipomas, pheo, mets. The MRI features are diagnostic here, but if in doubt, it should resolve or shrink in 6-8 weeks so follow-up is often reasonable.


Lastly, while bilateral adrenal hemorrhage can precipitate adrenal insufficiency -- it is very rare.

cases.clinicalHistory: Low attenuation right adrenal nodule on CT

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
4 cm right adrenal cystic lesion:

  • T2W fluid signal (similar to CSF), homogeneous, with thin/imperceptible wall
  • T1W hypointense
  • Post-gad images (not shown) demonstrated NO internal enhancement

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Adrenal cysts are very rare. Pathologically, they are separated into 4 groups: endothelial, pseudocyst (often post hemorrhage), epithelial (true cysts), and parasitic cysts (i.e. hydatid).


To call something a 'cyst' we must confirm lack of internal solid enhancement (i.e. tumors with cystic change should not be called 'cysts'. Cysts may be multilocular or have thicker walls (i.e. hydatid), or calcifications. In our case, we have a simple appearing cyst.


On non-contrast CT, a cyst may mimic a lipid-rich adenoma (homogeneous <10 HU). Of course, on CT and MRI, post-contrast images will show no true enhancement within in a cyst (unlike adenomas). The appearance on MRI is diagnostic.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Right adrenal lesion:

  • T2W hypointense
  • T1W hyperintense
  • No internal enhancement (confirmed on subtraction images)
  • Barely perceptible wall

Findings in keeping with an adrenal cyst with internal hemorrhagic/proteinaceous material.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html: We introduced adrenal cysts in the last case. This one has internal hemorrhagic / proteinaceous material (hence T1 hyperintensity, T2 hypointensity).
